matched_cl_name: Grant v. Raymond
gold_cite: Shaw v. Cooper, 32 U.S. 292 (1833)
proposition: “The policy of granting exclusive privileges in certain cases, was deemed of so much importance in a national point of view, that power was given to congress in the federal constitution.”
opus_60k_rationale: Marshall's opinion notes that promoting useful arts is a constitutional aim and quotes the patent/copyright clause, supporting the general idea that exclusive privileges were deemed important enough to be given to Congress, but the case does not frame this as a holding about the policy of granting exclusive privileges per se.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The opinion explicitly states at paragraph 134 that promoting progress of useful arts 'entered into the views of the framers of our constitution' and that the power to secure exclusive rights to inventors 'is among those expressly given to congress,' directly supporting the proposition.

matched_cl_name: Lexmark Int'l, Inc. v. Static Control Components, Inc.
gold_cite: Certified Pub. Accts. v. I.R.S., 804 F.3d 1193 (2015)
proposition: explaining that the zone of interests test must be treated procedurally like any other “non- jurisdictional issue”
opus_60k_rationale: Lexmark holds the zone-of-interests test is a statutory cause-of-action question, not jurisdictional, but it does not frame the test as a 'non-jurisdictional issue' to be treated procedurally like any other such issue.
sonnet_ft_rationale: Footnote 4 of the opinion explicitly states that the zone-of-interests test 'does not implicate subject-matter jurisdiction' and that, 'like any other element of a cause of action, it must be adequately alleged at the pleading stage,' directly supporting the proposition that it is a non-jurisdictional issue to be treated procedurally like other such issues.

matched_cl_name: Nautilus, Inc. v. Biosig Instruments, Inc.
gold_name: Festo Corp. v. Shoketsu Kinzoku Kogyo Kabushiki Co.
gold_cite: Festo Corp. v. Shoketsu Kinzoku Kogyo Kabushiki Co., 535 U.S. 722 (2014)
proposition: explaining that the definiteness “requirement is not a demand for unreasonable precision” and that “verbal precision” in every case is not possible “in a patent system that actually works, in practice”
opus_60k_rationale: Nautilus acknowledges language's inherent limitations and that absolute precision is unattainable, supporting the general idea, but the specific quoted phrases about 'unreasonable precision' and 'verbal precision...in a patent system that actually works' do not appear in this excerpt.
sonnet_ft_rationale: Nautilus does hold that absolute precision is unattainable and that some uncertainty is acceptable, supporting the general concept, but the specific quoted phrases 'not a demand for unreasonable precision' and 'verbal precision' and 'in a patent system that actually works, in practice' do not appear in the opinion excerpt and appear to be misattributed language, possibly from a different case or the Federal Circuit's remand opinion.

matched_cl_name: Dawson Chemical Co. v. Rohm & Haas Co.
gold_cite: Acumed LLC v. Stryker Corp., 551 F.3d 1323 (2008)
proposition: “The essential attribute of a patent grant is that it provides a right to exclude competitors from infringing the patent”
opus_60k_rationale: The opinion discusses the right to exclude as central to patent grants (e.g., quoting Carbice on the patentee's monopoly and discussing § 271(d)'s 'limited power to exclude'), but the case's main holding concerns patent misuse and contributory infringement, not a direct articulation of the proposition.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The case states 'the essence of a patent grant is the right to exclude others from profiting by the patented invention' (citing 35 U.S.C. § 154), which supports the core concept but uses different framing — the proposition's 'exclude competitors from infringing' is a slight paraphrase that conflates the right to exclude with the act of infringement, whereas the case's formulation focuses on profiting from the invention.

matched_cl_name: Cantu v. Resolution Trust Corp.
gold_cite: Nw. Airlines, Inc. v. Camacho, 296 F.3d 787 (2002)
proposition: finding that a claim for abuse of process is subject to California’s personal injury statute of limitations
opus_60k_rationale: The opinion expressly holds that abuse of process is an injury to the person subject to the one-year personal injury statute of limitations under Code Civ. Proc. § 340(3).
sonnet_ft_rationale: The opinion explicitly holds at *886-887 that 'abuse of process is an injury to the person' and therefore subject to the one-year statute of limitations under Code of Civil Procedure § 340, subd. (3), directly supporting the proposition.

matched_cl_name: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc.
gold_cite: Bennett v. Schmidt, 153 F.3d 516 (1998)
proposition: contrasting the standard for resolving a motion to dismiss with the standard on summary judgment
opus_60k_rationale: The opinion sets out the summary judgment standard and analogizes it to directed verdict, but does not actually contrast it with the motion-to-dismiss standard.
sonnet_ft_rationale: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby addresses summary judgment standards exclusively; it does not contrast the motion to dismiss standard with the summary judgment standard.

matched_cl_name: Zenith Radio Corp. v. Hazeltine Research, Inc.
gold_cite: Pate v. City of Sesser, 393 N.E.2d 1146 (1979)
proposition: “A release extinguishes a cause of action whereas a covenant not to sue affects only the right to bring suit and not the cause of action itself.”
opus_60k_rationale: The case acknowledges the common-law rule that a release extinguishes the cause of action but expressly repudiates it, and does not articulate the release/covenant-not-to-sue distinction stated in the proposition.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The excerpt explicitly states the ancient common-law rule that 'a release extinguished the cause of action to which it related' releases all joint tortfeasors, contrasting it with other rules, directly articulating the release/cause-of-action distinction the proposition describes; however, the case does not separately articulate the covenant-not-to-sue side of the proposition, making this a partial but sufficiently direct support for the release half of the claim.

matched_cl_name: Northington v. Jackson
gold_cite: Benefield v. McDowall, 241 F.3d 1267 (2001)
proposition: “[L]abeling an inmate a snitch has the potential for great harm and may violate constitutional guarantees.”
opus_60k_rationale: The court held that a guard labeling an inmate a 'snitch,' resulting in beatings by other inmates, may state an Eighth Amendment violation, directly supporting the proposition.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The case addresses a guard labeling an inmate a 'snitch' causing beatings as a potential Eighth Amendment violation, which is on point, but the proposition's quoted language ('has the potential for great harm and may violate constitutional guarantees') does not appear verbatim in the excerpt — the court's actual holding is narrower, finding only that the allegations *may* state an Eighth Amendment claim under a failure-to-protect theory, not a broader constitutional pronouncement about snitch-labeling generally.

matched_cl_name: Michael Massey and John Otten, M.D. v. David Helman
gold_cite: Cicchetti v. Lucy, 514 F.2d 362 (1975)
proposition: “[A]n amended complaint normally is treated as completely replacing the former pleading, even the residual claim for damages disappeared . . . when the district court granted the motion to amend.”
opus_60k_rationale: The case states an amended complaint supersedes prior pleadings (Carver citation), supporting the general principle, but does not specifically address the residual-damages-claim disappearance language in the proposition.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The case supports the general principle that an amended complaint supersedes prior pleadings (para. 35), but the quoted proposition's specific language about a 'residual claim for damages disappear[ing] when the district court granted the motion to amend' is not found in this excerpt — the case addresses waiver of affirmative defenses, not disappearance of damages claims upon amendment.

matched_cl_name: Law Offices of David Efron v. Matthews & Fullmer Law Firm
gold_cite: Wiener v. MIB Grp., Inc., 86 F.4th 76 (2023)
proposition: “It is well-accepted that federal courts may take judicial notice of proceedings in other courts if those proceedings have relevance to the matters at hand.” (quoting Law Offices of David Efron v. Matthews & Fullmer Law Firm, 782 F.3d 46, 56 n.7 (1st Cir. 2015))
opus_60k_rationale: The cited case contains the exact quoted language in footnote 7, accurately attributed and supporting the proposition about judicial notice of other court proceedings.
sonnet_ft_rationale: Footnote 7 of the opinion quotes verbatim the proposition at issue, citing Kowalski v. Gagne, 914 F.2d 299, 305 (1st Cir. 1990), confirming both the language and the cited case are accurate.

matched_cl_name: Neal v. Shimoda
gold_cite: Dongarra v. Smith, 27 F.4th 174 (2022)
proposition: “It is obvious that branding [the prisoner plaintiff] a sex offender could make him a target of prison violence.”
opus_60k_rationale: The Ninth Circuit's opinion explicitly discusses the stigmatizing consequences of the sex offender label, supporting that such branding could expose a prisoner to harm including violence.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The Neal v. Shimoda excerpt addresses stigmatizing consequences of the sex offender label in the context of due process, parole eligibility, and ex post facto claims — it contains no language about the label making an inmate a target of prison violence by fellow prisoners.

matched_cl_name: United States v. Serge Edouard
gold_cite: United States v. Moore, 651 F.3d 30 (2011)
proposition: “When evidence of such acts is intrinsic to the charged crime, it is not evidence of other acts and is thus wholly unregulated by Rule 404(b).” (cleaned up)
opus_60k_rationale: The opinion explicitly holds that intrinsic evidence—uncharged acts inextricably intertwined with or completing the story of the charged offense—falls outside Rule 404(b)'s scope.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The case confirms intrinsic evidence falls outside Rule 404(b)'s scope, but explicitly states such evidence 'must still satisfy the requirements of Rule 403,' making the proposition's claim that it is 'wholly unregulated' an overstatement.

matched_cl_name: Weinstein v. Islamic Republic of Iran
proposition: holding that “[b]y its terms, [TRIA] § 201 does not provide for execution against assets that are not blocked”
opus_60k_rationale: The case repeatedly emphasizes that TRIA § 201(a) authorizes execution against 'blocked assets,' implying non-blocked assets are not reachable, but it does not contain the quoted holding or squarely decide that limitation.
sonnet_ft_rationale: Weinstein v. Islamic Republic of Iran, 609 F.3d 43 holds that TRIA § 201 does provide jurisdiction for attachment of blocked assets of instrumentalities; it does not contain or endorse the quoted proposition that § 201 does not provide for execution against unblocked assets.

matched_cl_name: Lukovsky v. City and County of San Francisco
gold_cite: City of Los Angeles, 471 F. App’x 620 (2012)
proposition: noting that “California has the 2 “discovery rule” of tolling, “which postpones accrual of a cause of action until the plaintiff 3 discovers, or has reason to discover, the cause of action.
opus_60k_rationale: The case discusses the federal discovery rule for civil rights claim accrual, not California's discovery rule specifically; it notes the rule is incorporated into federal accrual law without endorsing the proposition's California-specific framing.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The case mentions the 'discovery rule' framing in the context of plaintiffs' argument, but the court's actual holding applies federal accrual law (not California's discovery rule) and rejects the broader reading plaintiffs sought, making the citation a partial rather than direct support for California's discovery rule tolling proposition.

matched_cl_name: Virginia v. Moore
gold_name: Atwater v. City of Lago Vista
gold_cite: Atwater v. City of Lago Vista, 532 U.S. 318 (2001)
proposition: reserving the question of whether “the Fourth Amendment entails an ‘in the presence’ requirement for purposes of misdemeanor arrests”
opus_60k_rationale: Moore repeatedly references the 'in the presence' formulation when describing the probable-cause arrest rule but does not squarely decide or expressly reserve whether the Fourth Amendment requires presence for misdemeanor arrests, so it only weakly supports the reservation proposition.
sonnet_ft_rationale: Moore does not reserve the 'in the presence' question; it affirmatively holds that warrantless arrests for crimes committed in the officer's presence are Fourth Amendment-reasonable, without flagging the non-in-presence scenario as an open question.

matched_cl_name: Rite-Hite Corp. v. Kelley Co.
gold_cite: Acumed LLC v. Stryker Corp., 551 F.3d 1323 (2008)
proposition: “The essential attribute of a patent grant is that it provides a right to exclude competitors from infringing the patent”
opus_60k_rationale: The opinion repeatedly characterizes the patent right as the right to exclude others from making, using, or selling the invention, directly supporting the proposition.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The opinion explicitly states 'it is a clear purpose of the patent law to redress competitive damages resulting from infringement of the patent' and repeatedly frames the patent grant as conferring the right to exclude others from making, using, or selling the patented invention, directly supporting the proposition.

matched_cl_name: Celsis in Vitro, Inc. v. CellzDirect, Inc.
gold_name: Celsis In Vitro, Inc. v. CellzDirect, Inc.
gold_cite: Celsis In Vitro, Inc. v. CellzDirect, Inc., 664 F.3d 922 (2012)
proposition: affirming a district court’s finding of irreparable harm when the testimony included evidence that a patentee was “precluded from marketing to potential and existing customers that it [was] the exclusive market leader”
opus_60k_rationale: The opinion expressly affirms irreparable harm based on expert testimony that Celsis was precluded from marketing to potential and existing customers that it is the exclusive market leader.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The opinion expressly states that Celsis' expert 'testified to the difficulty in quantifying the effect on reputation and business due to Celsis being precluded from marketing to potential and existing customers that it is the exclusive market leader,' and the Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's irreparable harm finding on that basis.

matched_cl_name: Thompson v. Paul
proposition: “[Plaintiffs] waited until they lost before asking for certification. This is almost always fatal unless the court sees strong policy reasons to insist on certification itself.”
opus_60k_rationale: The case explicitly establishes a presumption against certification when sought only after an adverse district court ruling, supporting the proposition that post-loss certification requests are almost always denied.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The excerpt explicitly states there is a presumption against certifying after an adverse ruling because a party should not get 'a second chance at victory,' and cites multiple circuits for the proposition that post-loss certification requests 'should be discouraged' absent sound discretion to certify.

matched_cl_name: Renchenski v. Williams
gold_cite: Dongarra v. Smith, 27 F.4th 174 (2022)
proposition: “It is obvious that branding [the prisoner plaintiff] a sex offender could make him a target of prison violence.”
opus_60k_rationale: The opinion expressly notes that sex offenders are targets of savage beatings and sexual violence in prison, directly supporting the proposition that branding an inmate a sex offender exposes him to prison violence.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The case discusses stigmatizing consequences and that sex offenders face 'unique challenges in the prison environment' including being targets for sexual violence, but the specific quoted proposition about 'branding' making a prisoner 'a target of prison violence' does not appear verbatim; the case's actual language references beatings and sexual abuse rates among sex offenders generally, not the precise framing stated in the proposition.

matched_cl_name: Mashpee Tribal Cncl v. Norton, Gale A.
gold_name: Mashpee Wampanoag Tribal Council, Inc. v. Norton
gold_cite: Mashpee Wampanoag Tribal Council, Inc. v. Norton, 336 F.3d 1094 (2003)
proposition: “[The rule of reason] cannot be decided in the abstract, by reference to some number of months or years beyond which agency inaction is presumed to be unlawful but will depend in large part . . . upon the complexity of the task at hand, the significance (and permanence
opus_60k_rationale: The opinion expressly states the rule of reason cannot be decided in the abstract by reference to some number of months or years but depends on the complexity of the task, significance and permanence of the outcome, and agency resources.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The excerpt contains the verbatim language of the proposition at pages 10-11: 'That issue cannot be decided in the abstract, by reference to some number of months or years beyond which agency inaction is presumed to be unlawful, but will depend in large part, as we have said, upon the complexity of the task at hand, the significance (and permanence) of the outcome, and the resources available to the agency.'

matched_cl_name: Platten v. HG Bermuda Exempted Ltd.
gold_cite: Lipsitt v. Plaud, 994 N.E.2d 777 (2013)
proposition: noting that the Supreme Judicial Court “recognized” these factors in Attorney General v. M.C.K., Inc., 432 Mass. 546, 555 & n.19, 736 N.E.2d 373, 380–81 & n.19 (2000)
opus_60k_rationale: The opinion at 736 N.E.2d 373, 380 n.19 explicitly cites and recognizes the twelve enumerated factors (the 'Pepsi-Cola factors') governing corporate disregard, supporting the proposition.
sonnet_ft_rationale: The Platten opinion directly cites Attorney Gen. v. M.C.K., Inc., 736 N.E.2d 373, 380 n.19 (Mass. 2000) as the source for the twelve corporate-disregard factors, confirming that the SJC recognized those factors there.

matched_cl_name: Law Offices of David Efron v. Matthews & Fullmer Law Firm
gold_cite: Wiener v. MIB Grp., Inc., 86 F.4th 76 (2023)
proposition: “It is well-accepted that federal courts may take judicial notice of proceedings in other courts if those proceedings have relevance to the matters at hand.” (quoting Law Offs. of David Efron v. Matthews & Fullmer L. Firm, 782 F.3d 46, 56 n.7 (1st Cir. 2015))
opus_60k_rationale: The opinion's footnote 7 contains the exact quoted language supporting the proposition about judicial notice of other court proceedings.
sonnet_ft_rationale: Footnote 7 of the opinion quotes Kowalski v. Gagne, 914 F.2d 299, 305 (1st Cir. 1990) for the exact proposition, word-for-word, and the citation matches 782 F.3d 46 at page 56 n.7.
